data.title: Howmet Aerospace: Flying Too High, Some Turbulence To Be Expected
data.description:
Shares of Howmet Aerospace (HWM) have been on a huge momentum run in recent times, with volatility being on the increase as of late. Shares are down 15% from the highs, but continue to trade at real elevated multiples, following strong growth reported here, with Howmet reporting some interesting M&A deals here.

While I like the dealmaking, given the relative valuations and the organic performance, momentum has been too strong to see real appeal. However, I am glad to continue to cover shares here, amidst corporate developments taking place.

Other, higher conviction ideas, including secular growth plays in industrial and aerospace segments, can be found at Value In Corporate Events.

Taking Advantage Of Stanley's Woes

Towards year end, Howmet announced a $1.8 billion cash deal to acquire Consolidated Aerospace Manufacturing, a designer, and manufacturer of precision fasteners, fluid fittings, and other complex and highly-engineered products for aerospace and defense applications. The deal results in some significant, yet unknown, tax benefits.

The deal is seen contributing about $490 million in sales in 2026, with EBITDA margins seen exceeding 20%; that is before synergies. That suggests that EBITDA is seen around $100 million on a standalone basis, as the effective purchase price drops to 13 times, factoring in unknown costs and tax synergies.

That reveals to me that the deal comes at a price of around 3.6 times sales, about 18 times EBITDA, and about 13 times EBITDA, once tax and cost synergies are accounted for. These are not necessarily dirt-cheap multiples, but in the grand scheme of aerospace valuations, they look relatively compelling.

Adding To An Aerospace Empire

In February, Howmet reported its 2025 results. Revenues rose by 11% to $8.25 billion that year, with the business being very profitable, posting GAAP operating earnings of $2.05 billion for margins equivalent to a quarter of sales. Net earnings of $1.51 billion come in at $3.71 per share on a diluted basis, with adjusted earnings coming in six cents higher, all of that looking quite clean.

That business is well diversified. Engine products make up about half of sales, these being very lucrative sales with EBITDA margins reported at a third of sales. This is accompanied by a $1.7 billion fastening systems segment, these being very lucrative activities as well, with margins reported around 30% of sales. Both engineered structured and forged wheels are about a billion-dollar business with margins reported in the twenties.

Trading around a high of $260 per share, the equity valuation peaked around $104 billion, which, combined with a net debt load of $2.3 billion, makes for a $106 billion enterprise valuation. This values the business at around 13 times sales, making the purchase of Stanley's assets look like a steal, with the recent deal adding about 6% to pro forma sales. Needless to say, from a valuation perspective, shares traded at a very demanding 70 times earnings multiple.

This comes after shares have taken off over the past decade; this was just a $30 stock a decade ago, in fact trading at similar levels as recently as 2022. Shares broke the $100 mark towards year-end 2024, with incredible momentum seen ever since.

The company guided for a continuation of growth, with 2026 sales seen at a midpoint of $9.10 billion, with sales seen up around 10%. Adjusted EBITDA is seen around $2.76 billion, with earnings seen up around a point to just over 30% of sales. All this should result in earnings advancing further to $4.45 per share, marking real leverage, with earnings multiples seen down to 58 times based on the guidance, although that was applied to the peak valuation at $260 per share. Shares have now pulled back to $228 per share, as valuations remain demanding at around 51 times earnings.

From the outlook and conference call, it was not obvious if the guidance included the Consolidated deal, or not, but common practice is that it does not. If that is the case, some real accretion could be seen, at least in terms of sales, but limited immediate earnings accretion in the near term given additional financing costs to be incurred.

Furthermore, the company announced a deal for Brunner, valued at $120-$150 million, adding about $60 million in sales. That adds nearly a percent to overall sales, taking place at a compelling 2.0-2.5 times sales multiple.

With pro forma net debt seen around $4.2 billion, leverage is far from an issue, with EBITDA seen around $2.8 billion based on the guidance for the year and recent M&A contribution. This provides a roadmap for earnings to advance to $5 per share, at least as a run rate by year-end, as even such achievements translate into a 45 times earnings multiple here, while leverage is seen around 1.5 times.

What Now?

The reality is that the valuations are very demanding, as has been the case for a while now. Aerospace at large has been on a massive run recently, yet current events cause some concerns here amidst sky-high valuations. Part of this re-rating has also been driven by a thriving gas turbine unit, with the company being a derivative of the data center boom as well.

However, the company is of real quality, as that is just what has been priced into the stock, with the track record being strong, although aided by the positioning. While the company has seen some real advancements in sales, margins, and a reduction in the share count, the share price advancement has far outpaced these earnings achievements on a per-share basis, making me naturally cautious.

Even with shares down 15% from the absolute highs, shares are seen back to levels last seen as recently as early February, with shares still having doubled over the past year or so. That actually followed shares having doubled in the year before, as an indication of the momentum seen. That is what makes me cautious, despite continued and bolt-on dealmaking at very compelling (relative) valuations here.

This makes me like the relative arbitrage of recent M&A, but the absolute valuations are very demanding, making me cautious here, as much lower valuations should be seen before I am willing to reconsider my stance.

data.title: Unilever: Portfolio Reshaping Advances With Food Business Under Review
data.description:
Introduction

The last time I covered Unilever (UL), I highlighted how their planned ice cream spin-off and 2030 plan could help improve their margins, although there were several macro headwinds to keep in mind.

Although the stock is down to 52-week lows following the escalation of the Iran conflict, broader macro pressure, and recently announcing their intention to look into a potential spin-off/sale of their food business, I believe UL’s current valuation remains fair, with the margin of safety not justifying a buy yet.

Internal Developments

UL reported a relatively solid quarter and year, with a 3.5% growth rate in underlying sales in 2025, 2 percentage points coming from price growth and 1.5 points from volume growth, the latter being quite rare in this environment, being an advantage of the company’s global footprint, while their 30 “Power Brands” (78% of turnover; 11 of them being billion-dollar brands) delivered even better numbers.

Meanwhile, the free cash flow (excluding the Magnum Ice Cream (MICC) branch that was successfully demerged recently - with UL keeping 19.9% of them, for now - reached €5.9 billion, with a 100% cash conversion rate, while they also completed several other portfolio dispositions and bolt-on acquisitions.

For 2026, Unilever expects a similar 4% to 6% underlying sales growth rate, with modest underlying operating margin improvements from last year’s 20% - which remains solid for a company in this industry, further highlighting their diversified, global operations with significant exposure to Asia (44% of their turnover, double the 22% coming from North America) - with CAPEX expected at about 3% of their turnover.

Financially, based on UL’s latest report, we can see an overall normal position for a company like this, with the current assets not covering the current liabilities, but with an overall manageable level of debt, with a relatively low 2.0x Net Debt/Underlying EBITDA, reducing their net debt by €1.4 billion following the demerger.

Unilever also announced a new €1.5 billion share buyback announced after spending €1.5 billion in 2025 as well, while the 3.77% dividend represents about €4.45 billion, for a combined yield of about 5.13% based on the current price and a combined payout of roughly 100% of the FCF. Note that this FCF is down compared to 2024 mostly due to costs associated with the Magnum demerger, meaning that their “real” cash flow would’ve been enough to support this yield.

Potential Food Spin-Off

Based on recent reports, Unilever is considering another spin-off following the Magnum one that was recently completed, this time for their legacy food business (about a quarter of turnaround, expected to be dethroned by personal care and beauty in the medium-term), although it’s still exploring ideas right now and we can’t know if it will be a full split or just a select number of brands - if any.

However, although a public spin-off may not happen immediately, the company already announced talks in this regard from companies like Kraft Heinz (KHC) and McCormick (MKC) - recently confirmed by Unilever in a press release - which can potentially result in some deal even this year given how much interest was seen in just a few days.

Although it’s early to conclude anything, this move is not the worst, as it can allow the company’s strong brands to shine in more concentrated entities instead of trading them at a “conglomerate discount,” separating the higher-growth beauty and personal care (which can also be bought by a giant in their respective industries if they want to sell everything) from the slower and mature food business.

However, this move also raises M&A-specific risks to keep in mind (e.g. eliminating synergies, weaker bargaining power), which likely had an impact on the market given the drop that came with the initial announcement. Not only that, but selling anything during an arguably weak environment can risk undervaluing it, as sales/cash flow/margins/etc. data that would go into determining the value of these assets today have been under significant pressure from the broader environment, potentially losing some value for the shareholders.

Far From The Best Macro Setting

Although we’re talking about a diversified, global giant, it’s important to note that the current environment is far from great, with significant issues and trade-offs/trade-downs reported by companies from virtually every industry. This is a normal effect of a high-rate environment, but the macro pressure from tariffs and now the Iran conflict added extra layers of uncertainty and stress. This geographic advantage was also highlighted by Unilever’s CFO during their Q4 Earnings Call, stating:

We delivered balanced growth across developed and emerging markets despite a more uneven macro and consumer backdrop through the year. This highlights the advantage of our geographic footprint. In developed markets, we grew ahead of our categories despite consumer conditions softening, particularly in the second half. In emerging markets, performance improved throughout the year, reflecting decisive actions we took to address challenges alongside improved execution, a step-up in innovation and a more focused channel execution as well as an improving trading environment in several key markets.

In fact, the latter can lead to significantly worse than just some consumer weakness, as it already has visible effects on fuel prices, with oil recently reaching nearly $120/bbl again following the recent escalations, which, alongside the rise in gas and fertilizer prices (since the region is key for these as well) can be the final drop for an already weak global economy, potentially leading to a recession.

Even though this company may seem relatively defensive, the ongoing trade-offs and pressure seen in recent years can prove quite the opposite, as even the products that are considered defensive can come in lower price or better value forms.

For this, retailers and their white labels are a big fundamental threat that can be boosted significantly by these trends, which not only limits the available shelf space and thus hurts the legacy brands’ power of negotiation, but it also generates immense amounts of valuable data. Once things improve, retailers such as Walmart (WMT), Kroger (KR), etc. can then offer better price points that would directly compete with the likes of Unilever, especially since they’d be able to capitalize on the “free” advertising they got for their private brands, with consumers trusting them even more (which is a very important aspect for the purchasing decision).

Once again, even though they are a global producer with strong brands, these long-term trends can cause fundamental disruptions, although it’s more likely to see companies with weaker brands that are easier to replace be affected more, at least at first.

Valuation

For UL’s FCF to Equity valuation, starting from their $6.844 billion in 2025 (converting everything into USD for this one), which I'll assume can grow to $7.5 billion in 2026 to take into account a reversal from this year’s taxes combined with their growth potential, without accounting for any change in net borrowings to highlight their pure cash flow generation potential. Note that I won’t account for any further spin-offs yet since it’s currently just an idea, and there’s no certainty that anything will happen, so far.

I’ll assume this can grow at a CAGR of 6% over the first 5 years, which is in line with their expected net sales growth rate, possible thanks to their portfolio reshaping initiatives on top of potential macro improvements and Magnum stake sellout to fund other initiatives, followed by a 3.5% CAGR for the subsequent 5 years and 2.75% terminal growth rate (slightly better thanks to their significant exposure to the higher-growth Asian market), with a discount rate of 10% to take into account the competition, macro trends and solid financials, as well as 2.18 billion shares outstanding as of their Q4 report.

This way, we get to an equity value of $117.73 billion, for an intrinsic value of $54 per share, which is below the current $61.47, while slightly better assumptions get us pretty much at today’s levels.

Conclusions

I reiterate UL’s Hold rating, as the valuation seems fair now and the macro environment has plenty of room to deteriorate, while broader macro trends and pressure can turn out to be significant headwinds for legacy brands, which is why I’ll continue to demand a better margin of safety especially since there are better options in today’s market for growth, value, dividends, or all three.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Digital Realty Trust: The Reasons I Am Staying On The Sidelines
data.description:
Owning an equity REIT, like Digital Realty Trust (DLR), could be lucrative to many investors who want to have exposure to the real estate market but do not want to commit to owning a property directly. It is even more lucrative, when the REIT is active in the data center space, which is currently experiencing significant secular tailwinds due to the huge CAPEX spending on AI infrastructure buildout. Last, but not least, DLR's steadily growing dividends over the past two decades could also be attractive for income-focused investors, especially as the firm's income stream is relatively independent from consumer sentiment, meaning that the dividends are not likely to be in danger, even if people stop spending on discretionary goods.

Like with every investment, an investment in DLR also comes with risks. First, DLR is heavily reliant on debt, meaning that if the interest rates were to stay elevated for longer, their cost of capital could also increase, putting downward pressure on the bottom line results. Also, the price we pay for the stock, significantly impacts our return potential for the future. I believe now DLR is not valued attractively enough to start a new position or to add to an existing one, based on a dividend discount model.

I started covering DLR in August 2023, with my last article having been published over a year ago in February 2025. Each time I wrote about the firm, valuation was raising concerns, despite the attractiveness of the business as a whole.

1. DLR is an attractive data center REIT

DLR is an attractive REIT right now as the demand for their property is strong, driven by the AI infrastructure build out ambitions. If you want to enter the real estate market, but you do not want to commit to buying a physical property, starting with investing in a REIT could be a viable option. And DLR's presence in the data center space is especially lucrative. They also have a large, diversified portfolio of properties, consisting of more than 300 data centers in more than 55 metro areas, serving over 5500 customers.

Relying on a large number of tenants over different geographic locations makes DLR less dependent on a single client or single revenue stream, making their business more robust. If any of the tenants were to default or if any of the geographic regions were to experience weaker demand, DLR could still survive and thrive elsewhere. And when I talk about different geographic locations, I am not only talking about the United States, but about the entire globe.

When it comes to growth, the firm plans to keep growing globally, expecting more than 5 GWs of future development capacity across the globe.

Further, the company's strong backlog also demonstrates the high demand for DLR's services, and also allows us to gauge how the rent revenue is likely to develop in the coming years. A total backlog of $1.4 billion is definitely a good sign.

All in all, I believe that DLR has a solid, well-diversified, growing business, supported by secular tailwinds. This is, however, not the full story. While one might think that all these justify a buy rating, there is much more to it - namely valuation and debt.

2. Valuation and Dividends

To estimate DLR's fair value, I am going to be using a dividend discount model today. Why is this model suitable? DLR has been a reliable dividend payer for more than two decades, and over this period they also managed to increase the payments significantly. REITs are actually obliged to pay out at least 90% of their annual taxable income to the shareholders as dividends to maintain their REIT status. Currently, the firm pays a quarterly dividend of $1.22 per share, equivalent to an annual yield of roughly 2.8%.

When it comes to dividend discount models, there are two key input parameters: required rate of return and dividend growth. For the required rate of return, I normally use the firm's cost of equity, which is estimated to be 7.34% currently for DLR.

For the dividend growth rate, I generally tend to use longer-term historic averages. In DLR's case it might not be a good idea. Their 10-year dividend CAGR is 3.59%, which would be a very aggressive assumption to use in perpetuity, especially when we consider that in the past years the growth significantly slowed.

For my first case, I will assume 0% dividend growth in perpetuity. This results in a fair value of $71 per share, which is less than 50% of the current market price. One might say that these assumptions may be too pessimistic, however.

So let us look at a more realistic case, and let us assume that DLR's dividend growth rate is going to be similar to the overall growth rate of the U.S. economy - 2.5% in perpetuity. In this case, the company's fair value would come in around $111 per share, still roughly 37% lower than the current share price.

So what is the stock really priced for, then? Even if I assume that the secular tailwinds due to the AI infrastructure buildout are going to benefit DLR's business significantly in the coming years, and assume a 10% dividend growth rate in the coming 5 years and 2.5% afterward in perpetuity, the fair value still comes in at $154 per share, more than 10% below the current market price.

If you believe, however, that sustained double-digit growth is feasible in the next couple of years, then it may be a buying opportunity for you. If the firm actually manages to achieve such high growth rates, it would not only allow them to increase the dividends at a higher pace, but it would also possibly warrant a multiple expansion, which could lead to a significant upside potential. This scenario may not be entirely impossible, as the firm's guidance for fiscal 2026 also assumes double digit growth across many metrics. The question is, how long the tailwinds are going to last, and how long DLR will be able to benefit from it at this rate. At this point, this thesis has too many uncertainties for my taste.

Last but not least, the fact that insiders have been only selling in the past years also does not convince me that the stock is attractively priced right now.

I believe that DLR is not attractive from a valuation point of view, considering the most recent dividend growth trends, and the stock price should significantly come down, before it could start becoming attractive for value focussed investors.

3. Debt

With the ongoing geopolitical tension in the Middle East, debt is starting to become a larger problem. The issue is primarily driven by the elevated energy prices. High-energy prices often lead to inflation, as the cost of transportation and manufacturing increases. Higher inflation means that the Fed is not likely to cut interest rates in order to tame inflation.

Higher Fed funds rates lead to higher borrowing costs and therefore higher cost of capital. DLR has significant debt in its capital structure and therefore high interest rates directly increase the risk of the investment.

While DLR is trying to match the duration of its assets and liabilities, in 2027 and 2028, they will have $4.9 billion maturing, a significant portion of it in USD. If interest rates were to remain elevated, the refinancing cost of these debts could be also high. How interest rates will develop, will be very much dependent on how long the war in the Middle East lasts.

The high oil price is not only problematic because of its potential impact on inflation and interest rates, but also because operating data centers is a very energy-intensive business. Higher energy prices higher operating costs lead to a significant negative impact on the tenants' financial performance.

Conclusions

While DLR's business appears attractive at first sight due to its large, diversified portfolio and the secular tailwinds provided by the significant spendings on AI infrastructure build out, there is much more to it.

DLR appears to be significantly overvalued according to my dividend discount models, leaving little upside potential from the current price levels. The insider selling activity also leaves little room for optimism.

The firm's high reliance on debt could also be problematic, interest rates were to remain higher for longer, as a significant portion of DLR's debt is maturing in 2027 and 2028. High energy prices could also put downward pressure on the tenants' financial performance as data centers are very energy intensive.

All in all, I do not think it is attractive to start a new position in DLR now, or to add to an existing one.

data.title: Aveanna Healthcare Holdings: Recent Results Have Me More Optimistic (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
This article serves as an update to my previous analysis of Aveanna Healthcare Holdings (AVAH). When I last wrote about the company in July of 2025, I rated its stock as a Hold citing tight margins and potential challenges ahead from government payment for healthcare services. Fast forward to today and AVAH has gained a whopping 60.6% in the last seven and a half months since that article was published on Seeking Alpha. The source for most of these gains in the stock came after a quarterly report on August 7 buoyed AVAH over 50% in the next two trading days. On August 11, the company's rating was bumped up to Outperform by Raymond James. On September 11, it earned an upgrade from Underweight to Neutral from JP Morgan.

Aveanna Healthcare has experienced a very different market reaction to its Q4 2025 results, which were released on March 19. Its stock fell over 10% on the news even though the company reported GAAP earnings from the quarter of $0.80 per share, $0.65 better than expectations. The drag on the stock price came from the company’s projections for next year that included a disappointing revenue outlook. A graph that shows the performance of AVAH over the last 12 months is shown below.

What should be made from the last year’s roller coaster ride for AVAH? Did the market overreact when the stock rose significantly in August and September? Or are potential buyers being too pessimistic now? When everything is put together over the last 12 months, Aveanna Healthcare ended up with a total return that was just a few percentage points ahead of the S&P 500, once dividends are included.

Based on the most recent financial data available for the company, I am a now more optimistic for AVAH's future than I was last July. Aveanna Healthcare has made some noticeable improvements in its overall profitability, and it is growing in healthcare markets that are likely to continue to see high demand in the year ahead. At a time when so many other stocks are being impacted by higher energy prices and possible slowdown in economic growth, the company's home healthcare services should be largely unaffected by those concerns and provide some diversification from other, more cyclical investments. I am raising my assessment of the stock to a Buy, but this is not a rating that comes without significant risk, as I will discuss in my analysis below.

Company Overview

Aveanna Healthcare Holdings is a provider of a range of home-based healthcare services. The company cares for patients and families in 38 states through its network of over 360 office locations. The bulk of its operations take place in the Southeast and along the East Coast. A map of the company’s service area with additional information about its operations can be seen below.

Aveanna Healthcare Holdings separates its operations into three divisions when reporting its financial results. Those three are as follows: Private Duty Services, Home Health and Hospice, and Medical Solutions. Private duty services, or PDS, is by far the most important for the company from a revenue perspective. It is this business segment that offers skilled private nursing, non-clinical support, and pediatric therapy. In FY 2025, PDS provided over 80% of the total revenue for the company. A breakdown of the revenue contribution from each segment along with their gross margins is given below.

FY 2025 Revenue

Even though it provides most of the company's revenues, PDS has the lowest level of gross margin for the company at 29.6%. This was an improvement of 2.4% from FY 2024. Each segment of the company’s business did improve its gross margins last year, although the increase from Home Health and Hospice was minimal.

Aveanna Healthcare acknowledges the importance of organic growth in its investor presentations. However, the company has also been active in the M&A market over the last year. In June of 2025, it completed the acquisition of Thrive Skilled Pediatric Care, an independent provider of home care for children with 23 locations in seven states. The value of that transaction, which was closed with both cash and stock, was $75 million. On March 12, the company announced an agreement to buy Family First Homecare, a pediatric home health care provider. The deal, which will be funded by a combination of cash on hand and debt, is valued at $175.5 million and is expected to close in Q2 2026.

AVAH does not pay a dividend, preferring to use any profits to invest in future growth for the company. As part of its latest 10-K filing, Aveanna Healthcare Holdings stated that it does not intend to pay any dividends for its common stock “for the foreseeable future”. This should not be a surprise to investors in the Health Care space as none of the top 10 companies from the sector that are currently rated on Seeking Alpha pay a dividend.

Analysis of Aveanna Healthcare Holdings

A snapshot of the company’s Q4 2025 financial performance can be seen below. When compared with Q4 2024, overall revenue for the company increased $142 million to $662 million. Overall gross margins in the quarter actually declined from 33.0% to 32.2%, even as they expanded for the full year. It was the bottom line performance of AVAH that may have captured the most attention from shareholders as the company revealed that its net income per diluted share totaled $0.80 in Q4 2025, a $0.64 improvement over the same period in 2024.

The adjusted EPS for the company in the last quarter was just $0.17, however. The company booked a $139 million income tax benefit in Q4 2025. When this benefit is combined with several non-recurring costs related to merger activity, net income for the quarter dropped from $178.8 million to just $38.5 million. That is still over three times what was earned in Q4 2024, but it is a substantial change in the reported performance of the company.

The biggest reason why the market reacted the way that it did to the Q4 earnings report was not the profit adjustments but the company’s outlook for the year ahead. Aveanna Healthcare announced that its revenue for FY 2026 would likely fall in the range of $2.54 billion to $2.56 billion. Analysts were projecting $2.56 billion, so the company’s estimates fell below that mark at their midpoint. AVAH's estimated adjusted EBITDA average of $320 for FY 2026 was just ahead of analysts' estimate of $318 million. At a time when the market seemed to be looking for any reason to put selling pressure on stocks, in my opinion, AVAH simply fell victim to last week’s overall market negatively.

Profit Growth Appears Sustainable

Back in FY 2023, AVAH reported a loss of $0.12 per share on a normalized basis. In FY 2024, the company was able to report a profit of $0.05 per share. Even when the aforementioned adjustments are made, Aveanna Heathcare made a sizeable leap forward to $0.60 per share in FY 2025. AVAH currently trades at a forward PE of 10.31, well below the median for its sector.

The company's EBITDA estimates for FY 2026 were slightly better than what the market expected, and market conditions seem to support further expansion. The home healthcare market is one that should see sustained growth, not just in 2026, but over the next several years. Some projections show the home health care market rising by just over 12% annually for the next decade. Pediatric home health care, an area where AVAH has shown a track record of success, is not growing quite as quickly, but it has not faced the same regulatory scrutiny for fraud as the adult market in the last year. The lower costs associated with home care support produce fewer headwinds from overall rising health care costs.

The stated strategic initiatives for AVAH in 2026 include strengthening partnerships with government partners and preferred payers to create additional capacity and growth. In 2025, the company was able to advance its legislative agenda in 10 states to improve reimbursement rates for its services. Another initiative for the company is continuing efforts to lower the cost of care and implement what it calls "high-priority artificial intelligence and automation" to improve its operational efficiency. Additional M&A activity, even after the Family First deal is complete, is also likely even as the company strives to improve its net leverage and continue to generating positive free cash flow.

Additional Risks to Consider

The debt load from the company is a concern, and its ability to repay this debt is in question based on its recent debt rating. Aveanna Healthcare’s most recent credit rating update from Fitch was delivered on September 18. A rating of “B-” was assigned to AVAH’s most recent first lien term loan. This means that the company’s debt is considered highly speculative and below investment grade. A full picture of Aveanna Healthcare’s capital structure including the company’s capacity to draw additional funds from a $225.5 million revolver is shown below.

As part of its credit assessment, Fitch made the following statement:

Aveanna's business is highly exposed to changes in federal Medicaid funding. The Congressional Budget Office expects the recent U.S. tax and spending bill to lower federal Medicaid funding by over $1 trillion over the next 10 years. About 58% of company revenues are from Medicaid Managed Care Organization payors and 23% are directly from Medicaid.

It was noted that the company’s operations with pediatric patients should insulate revenue and margins from larger impacts, as these patients are less impacted by qualifications for benefits related to work requirements. Home health demand is higher since it provides lower cost options for patients than hospital care. Nonetheless, the company’s exposure to regulatory risks is significant, and the current presidential administration has made Medicare spending a target for potential future budget cuts.

In my previous analysis, I noted that the company faced integration risks because of its acquisition of Thrive Skilled Pediatric Care. The same can be said now with its additional of Family First Homecare. This transaction has the added concern of the additional debt required for the purchase, which could related back to the first risk mentioned above. There are also no guarantees that expected synergies from Family First will be achieved. The benefits for the company’s bottom line may be less than expected.

Insider selling also appears to show some internal pessimism with a number of large transactions taking place since the start of this year. Much of this activity is tax-related as insiders sell to cover the consequences of their equity awards. Nonetheless, this is not the kind of chart that I like to see when raising my opinion on any stock.

The Bottom Line

When I rated AVAH as a Hold in my first analysis, I stated that I was close to referring to the stock as a “high-risk buy”. Now, I think there is enough support to upgrade my opinion, even as the debt load from the company is concerning. If the company can grow its earnings in FY 2026 even by a small amount, then the stock could be a bargain at its current multiple.

data.title: SouthState Bank: A Premium Sunbelt Franchise, But Macro Risks Keep Us On The Sidelines
data.description:
Thesis

SouthState Bank Corporation (SSB) is a growing regional bank that has demonstrated strong return metrics and sound balance sheet management, but mixed credit quality. The bank will likely continue to deliver solid performance in portfolios where it stands as a core position; however, the current macroeconomic environment and valuation of SSB make it a less than ideal time to enter into new positions in this regional bank. Consequently, I am issuing a hold rating on SSB.

Company Profile

SouthState Bank Corporation is headquartered in Winter Haven, FL, and provides financial services through its flagship subsidiary, SouthState Bank. The firm offers consumer, commercial, mortgage, and wealth management banking services. SouthState Bank operates in nine states, including Florida, Texas, North Carolina, South Carolina, Georgia, Colorado, Alabama, Virginia, and Tennessee. As of December 31, 2025, the company holds roughly $67 billion in assets and $55 billion in deposits.

Return Metrics

SSB has demonstrated above-average return metrics over the prior eight quarters, providing evidence that the firm’s strategy of organic expansion in high-growth markets is working. Return on Tangible Common Equity (ROTCE) is one of the most common return metrics in banking. This metric shows the bank’s net income relative to its average tangible common equity. Tangible common equity removes intangible assets like goodwill, which can heavily distort return metrics. Over the prior eight quarters, SSB has generated ROTCE in the double digits (note the dip in Q1, 2025 was a result of the acquisition of Independent Bank Group). Additionally, the bank’s ROTCE remains north of the peer median of 15% as of Q4, 2025.

Net Interest Income (NII), arguably the most important return metric for a bank, measures the difference between the interest the bank receives on its assets (loans and securities) versus the interest it pays out on its liabilities (mostly deposits). Over the prior eight quarters, SSB has grown NII an impressive 64%. It is noted that the large increase in NII from Q4, 2024 to Q1, 2025 was the result of the acquisition of Independent Bank Group.

Finally, net interest margin (NIM), another important return metric for banks, measures the net interest income generated versus the average earning assets (largely, loans and investments) used to obtain it. Over the prior eight quarters, SSB has produced NIM in the 3% to 4% range, demonstrating the bank is making sound use of its assets to generate income; the peer median for Q4, 2025, was 3.65%.

Balance Sheet

Turning to the balance sheet, the Common Equity Tier 1 ratio (CET1) measures the firm’s highest quality capital versus its risk-weighted assets. This ratio tells us the degree to which the bank can absorb losses without needing external support. Over the prior eight quarters, SSB has produced a CET1 ratio well above the 6.50% threshold needed to be considered “well-capitalized” by the U.S. Prompt Corrective Action framework. Note the dip in Q1, 2025 was caused by the acquisition of Independent Bank Group causing the denominator (risk-weighted assets) to rise by roughly $18.1 billion.

Additionally, the Tier 1 Leverage ratio is an additional metric that assesses a bank’s loss-absorbing capacity, except rather than using risk-weighted assets like the CET1 ratio, the Tier 1 Leverage ratio uses average total consolidated assets in the denominator, thus examining the equity cushion as a proportion of the bank’s total balance sheet. Over the prior eight quarters, SSB has maintained a Tier 1 Leverage ratio well above the 5.0% threshold needed to be considered well-capitalized. Again, note the dip in Q1, 2025 as a result of the acquisition of Independent Bank Group.

Credit Quality

SSB has demonstrated strong credit quality with the exception of one large write-off over the prior eight quarters. The Net Charge-Offs (NCO) to Total Average Loans ratio measures the proportion of loans written off by the bank versus the total average loans held on the balance sheet. This ratio has hovered mostly under 0.10% over the prior eight quarters. In other words, less than 1/10th of a percent of the bank’s loans are written off. Please note that the reported numbers for Q1, 2025, and Q2, 2025, were 0.38% and 0.21%, respectively, but these were a result of the charge-offs incurred due to the acquisition of Independent Bank Group. The ratio is being adjusted for these data points due to the acquired charge-offs not being representative of SouthState Bank’s management but rather Independent Bank Group’s management.

However, the jump in Q3, 2025 was due to one large credit loss of a commercial and industrial loan for $21.5 million. This charge-off is concerning as it was sudden and no prior reserve was set aside for it. Although management insists this was an isolated event with no similar credits in the portfolio, it does demonstrate that idiosyncratic events can bypass the early warning indicators set up by the bank.

An additional metric used to assess a bank’s credit quality is the ratio of the Allowance for Credit Losses (ACL) divided by the total Non-Performing Loans (NPL). This ratio tells us the cushion the bank has set aside for its troubled loans. The higher the ratio, the more the bank is able to withstand potential losses caused by these problem loans. SSB has consistently set aside roughly two times its balance of non-performing loans in allowance, which is a sound credit quality practice.

Valuation

For valuation, we will use price-to-tangible book value (P/TBV) and price-to-earnings (P/E) as backup metrics. P/TBV is the preferred metric for banks as it strips out intangible assets like goodwill, which can arise due to mergers and acquisitions rather than organic core operations. On a P/TBV level, SSB trades at a slight premium to the justified fair price when using the peer median P/TBV of 1.53 and the tangible book value of SSB of 56.27 as of Q4, 2025, which yields a justified price of $85.94. The current price of $88.18 sits at a 2.60% premium to the justified price.

On a P/E level, SSB trades at a discount to the justified price. An important note is that the P/E ratio can be heavily distorted in the banking industry due to an income statement item called the Provision for Credit Loss (PCL), which is a line item to account for defaulted loans. SSB trades at a 7.71% discount to the justified price of $95.55 when using the peer median P/E ratio of 9.67 and the most recent adjusted earnings per share of $2.47 as of Q4, 2025.

Taking the midpoint of the justified price using P/TBV and P/E, we arrive at a fair value of $96.07, which represents an 8.30% upside from the current price of $88.71 as of the time of this writing.

Macroeconomic Uncertainty

The current macroeconomic environment should be mentioned prior to making any recommendation on a cyclical firm like a regional bank. The heightened uncertainty from the Iran war, concerns around private credit, and the potential for stagflation (low growth, high inflation) make this a less than ideal time to enter into new positions on regional banks. Regional banks benefit from a steepening yield curve (short end falling, long end rising), which has been widely anticipated until recent months. The reason banks benefit from a steepening yield curve is because their funding costs from deposits are short-term in nature and fall when the short end of the yield curve falls. Conversely, banks’ assets are generally long-term in nature and provide higher yield when the long-end rises. If the Iran war, private credit, or a period of stagflation produces an inverted or flattening yield curve, this would harm SSB and regional banks in general. SSB is currently asset-sensitive, meaning it gains NII when rates rise and loses NII when rates fall. Although this will help counterbalance the negative effects of an unfavorable move in rates, the overall effect will still be adverse due to the macroeconomic shock.

Conclusion

SSB is an efficient financial institution with a solid footprint in rapidly growing markets of the United States. The bank has produced strong return metrics and maintains a healthy balance sheet, but the credit quality practices remain mixed. The current macroeconomic factors, including the war in Iran causing yield curve uncertainty, private credit concerns, and the potential for a stagflationary recession, make it a less than ideal time to enter into a new position at a regional bank, especially for the possibility of only an 8% return. Consequently, I am issuing a hold rating on SSB. I will reassess if the Fed engages in an aggressive rate-cutting cycle, geopolitical uncertainties resolve, or a more favorable price entry is provided.

data.title: What Active REIT Managers Bought And Sold In Q4 2025
data.description:
Co-written by Austin Rogers for High Yield Landlord

As we have explained in the past, we don't make buying or selling decisions based on what any other investors are doing, including the best active REIT portfolio managers.

That said, we are very interested in what the REIT "smart money" is doing. It represents one data point among many that we consider in our research process.

Of course, most of the time, we do not have access to active REIT managers' commentary or thinking on why they made buy and sell decisions. This limits the value of seeing their quarterly trades. We can only guess, in most cases, about why any given active manager made the decisions they made during the last quarter.

However, some useful information and themes still emerge from poring over active REIT managers' 13F filings, so let's jump in and see what we can learn.

The latest filings show position sizes and trades as of the last quarter.

Starwood Capital Group Management

Billionaire Barry Sternlicht's Starwood Capital Group is mostly focused on direct property investments, but several years ago, the company began scooping up shares of deeply undervalued REITs and now maintains a small, concentrated REIT portfolio. Here is what he said in a past CNBC interview:

"By the way, when credit comes back, you are gonna see REITs take off... There are some unbelievable bargains in REITs. We did the same thing during the pandemic. We bought a dozen stocks all over the world and we had a 70% IRR on that stuff. We are already buying some stuff in the public market..." Barry Sternlicht, CEO/Chairman, Starwood

This small group of REITs gives a view into what Barry Sternlicht finds attractive on the public markets.

As you can see, Starwood's primary bet is on Sunbelt apartments, as over 2/3rds of the REIT portfolio is invested in the three big Sunbelt multifamily REITs: Camden Property Trust (CPT), Mid-America Apartment Communities (MAA), and NexPoint Realty Trust (NXRT).

In interviews as well as quarterly earnings conference calls, Sternlicht has repeatedly articulated his long-term bullishness on Sunbelt apartments based on the rapid drop in new supply over the next few years.

However, Starwood seems to be bullish on multifamily in general, given its exposure to Class A coastal apartment landlord Equity Residential (EQR) and Northeast-focused Veris Residential (VRE).

Lastly, we note that Starwood continues to hold one of our favorite industrial REITs, First Industrial Realty (FR).

Land & Buildings Investment Management

Jonathan Litt is an activist REIT investor and the founder of Land & Buildings.

Although we have at times disagreed with Litt in the past, we highly respect him as a thought leader in the public real estate space. Therefore, we pay close attention to what his investment company is buying and selling. While L&B does engage in shareholder activism for a number of its holdings, the company also seems to be a silent investor for others.

We note that, like Starwood, L&B's second-largest position is First Industrial (FR), which is also one of our largest holdings.

L&B's largest position is Centerspace (CSR), an owner of Class A and B apartments in the Mountain West region of the US (Colorado, Minnesota, Montana, North Dakota, and so on). This part of the country is typically overlooked by institutional investors and may hold hidden value. Perhaps L&B sees the potential for shareholder activism. That would explain its nearly 5% ownership stake in the REIT.

We note that L&B lightened up on its three senior housing REITs -- American Healthcare REIT (AHR), Ventas (VTR), and National Health Investors (NHI) -- in Q4 while adding to its positions in First Industrial, Equinix (EQIX), Sun Communities (SUI), American Homes 4 Rent (AMH), and Outfront Media (OUT), among others.

L&B continues buying Six Flags (FUN), but so far there are no signs that their quest to trigger a real estate spinoff is going to come to fruition. We will share our thoughts on the situation soon in a separate article here at High Yield Landlord.

Also, L&B opened a decent-sized position in National Storage Affiliates (NSA) in Q4, which has proven a smart move, as the stock price is up over 20% year-to-date as of February 18th.

L&B also opened a smaller position in Curbline Properties (CURB), a specialty retail REIT that owns small-format shopping centers with high frontage (visibility from the adjacent street).

In Q4, L&B also sold entirely out of their positions in Extra Space Storage (EXR), Crown Castle (CCI), Empire State Realty Trust (ESRT), AvalonBay Communities (AVB), and UDR Inc. (UDR).

Cohen & Steers

Cohen & Steers (CNS) is an asset management company focused on real assets, especially REITs, with $55.3 billion of assets under management.

Being such a large and well-respected specialist in publicly traded real estate securities, we naturally take a strong interest in how they structure their portfolio and what they're currently buying and selling.

Cohen & Steers' Top 20 Holdings:

Holdings #11-20 in the lower image above are interesting, because this mixture of mostly mid-cap REITs diverges from the passive index. We find that CNS added to blue-chip retail REITs like Kimco Realty (KIM) and Agree Realty (ADC) in Q4 while trimming Class A mall REIT Simon Property Group (SPG) and luxury resort landlord Host Hotels (HST).

Another interesting theme is that CNS doubled down on data center REITs Digital Realty (DLR), Equinix (EQIX), and Iron Mountain (IRM) on their dips during Q4, and they were also buying self-storage REITs like Extra Space Realty (EXR) and Public Storage (PSA).

But as you can see, Cohen & Steers' top 10 holdings don't stray terribly far from the largest market cap REITs in the passive index.

It's striking that top-tier senior housing REIT Welltower (WELL) accounts for over 11% of the total portfolio at a market value of $6.2 billion.

The sheer amount of capital under management by Cohen & Steers does constrain their top holdings to larger REITs. But the good news is that we know how CNS would structure a portfolio with much less capital because of its Real Estate Active ETF (CSRE), which has a mere $250 million in AUM.

Here are CSRE's top ten holdings:

With CSRE's ability to have large positions in smaller REITs, the ETF overweights National Storage Affiliates (NSA), Outfront Media (OUT), Essential Properties Realty Trust (EPRT), and Diamondrock Hospitality (DRH).

This reminds us of what Warren Buffett once said about how the size of Berkshire Hathaway (BRK.A) limited its investable universe. If he had less AUM to work with, Buffett would have been able to invest more heavily in small- and mid-cap companies.

And, we might add, this is a benefit of being an individual investor. We are able to take advantage of attractive opportunities among smaller market-cap REITs quite easily.

Let's focus on CNS's biggest buys (by share count) in Q4.

Cohen & Steers' Top Buys in Q4:

We were intrigued to see that four of our holdings at High Yield Landlord were top buys for CNS during Q4: Kite Realty (KRG), Crown Castle (CCI), Kimco, and Rayonier (RYN).

Clearly, CNS remains bullish about high-quality and well-located open-air retail real estate, as we are.

They also seem optimistic about Crown Castle's US wireless tower portfolio as well as its simplification and deleveraging plans this year.

Lastly, CNS must be at least somewhat bullish on the cyclical timber industry because of its addition of Rayonier.

Now, moving on to the top sells/trims during the quarter.

Cohen & Steers' Top Sells in Q4:

It appears as though Cohen & Steers prefers Diamondrock Hospitality over Host Hotels right now, which indicates company-specific preferences rather than a macro view on the hospitality sector.

CNS also trimmed its position in single-family rental REIT Invitation Homes (INVH), perhaps related to political risks. We are happy to see that, though, because it contributed to a lower stock price and gave us an even better entry point for INVH's high-quality and highly scaled portfolio.

CNS also trimmed its American Homes 4 Rent (AMH) position, which could simply indicate tax-loss selling at year-end.

CNS also significantly trimmed its position in VICI Properties (VICI) in Q4, which was a theme for active REIT investment managers during the quarter. This was likely related to concerns about Las Vegas foot traffic, financial softness in the middle class, and rumors about a potential rent reset from one of VICI's major tenants.

So far, though, this feels like a "sell the rumor" situation. Given the fact that VICI made it through the COVID-19 pandemic without rent reductions (and while collecting 100% of contractual rent!), we doubt that the much milder headwinds facing gaming operators today will result in material damage to VICI.

CenterSquare Investment Management

CenterSquare is another commercial real estate investment management company with $12.8 billion of AUM in public securities.

Given its fairly large REIT portfolio, CenterSquare unsurprisingly shares a decent amount of overlap with Cohen & Steers' portfolio, with Welltower acting as an anchor.

CenterSquare's Top 20 Holdings:

A few themes emerge here.

First, CenterSquare is evidently bullish on senior housing, data centers, self-storage, high-end retail, and single-family rentals.

Second, CenterSquare exhibited bullishness across all of its top 20 holdings in Q4, which signifies optimism over most areas of commercial real estate.

Top buys by share count include several High Yield Landlord holdings.

CenterSquare's Top Buys in Q4:

Class A mall landlord Macerich (MAC) was the top buy by share count, while Healthcare Realty (HR), Alexandria Real Estate (ARE), and Kimco also show up in this list.

The long-term outlook for healthcare-related names like HR and ARE is fairly clear, given aging demographics. And increasing exposure to MAC was probably related to the REIT's low valuation, the new CEO's promising turnaround plan, and the portfolio's exposure to the strong side of the K-shaped economy.

On the other hand, CenterSquare did engage in significant trimming and sales in Q4.

CenterSquare's Top Sells In Q4:

Notably, the firm sold most of its position in Healthpeak Properties (DOC), which may be a vote of no-confidence in CEO Scott Brinker's capital allocation prowess. Management's decision to sell medical outpatient properties to double down on beleaguered life science real estate is a risk that the market has so far received poorly.

Likewise, CenterSquare sold out of NNN REIT (NNN) while significantly trimming Federal Realty (FRT), Rexford Industrial (REXR), and VICI Properties, perhaps due to tax-loss harvesting. Whether these sales reflect CenterSquare's view of the REITs' fundamentals or are merely temporary trades is unknown.

Baron Real Estate Income Fund (BRIIX)

Here is one more active REIT manager we like to monitor. The Baron Real Estate Income Fund is the second-highest rated real estate fund by Morningstar, trailing only the non-income-focused Baron Real Estate Fund (BREFX).

Part of the secret of the Baron real estate funds' success has been their freedom to invest in real estate-adjacent spaces like alternative asset management (Brookfield Corporation) and real estate services firms (Jones Lang LaSalle Inc.). We also do that at High Yield Landlord with positions in companies like IWG and HASI.

Most of the portfolio, however, is invested in REITs. As you can see above, the Baron fund is heavily concentrated in industry bellwethers Prologis (PLD) and Welltower. And in general, Baron appears bullish on industrial real estate, senior housing, high-end retail, data centers, and wireless towers.

During Q4, Baron increased its position in senior housing, self-storage, timberland, and hospitality.

Meanwhile, Baron decreased its positions (or sold out entirely) in REITs that exhibited price weakness in late 2025, indicating tax-loss harvesting may have played a role in these trades.

In fact, in management's commentary following the quarter, they expressly stated that trimming AMT and selling IRM were motivated by short-term factors and that they may buy these REITs again in 2026.

While the commentary did not cover why Baron trimmed VICI, we will note here that all the major active REIT investment managers trimmed their positions in this REIT during Q4.

Without knowing their reasoning behind this, however, we can't say for sure what motivated this exodus.

Takeaways

The first takeaway we have from this analysis is that there is disagreement about particular REITs even within the "smart money" aka active managers. That reinforces the idea that no one active manager's buying or selling decision is enough to be really meaningful.

The second takeaway is that the REIT "smart money" is broadly bullish on senior housing, data centers, and higher-end retail. And there appears to be some rising optimism about self-storage. (That optimism for self-storage has been rewarded so far in 2026.)

Also, Healthcare Realty is getting more love from active managers now that the REIT switched CEOs, reduced its dividend, and initiated a smart turnaround plan.

The third takeaway is that while most of the top sales in Q4 were unique cases, there are a few themes. Office and hospitality both saw outsized selling in Q4, and VICI Properties was widely trimmed by active managers as well.

Meanwhile, Baron Funds and Cohen & Steers were selling single-family rentals in Q4 while Land & Buildings and CenterSquare were buying SFR REITs.

At High Yield Landlord, we will continue to monitor what the best active REIT managers are doing as we manage our active portfolio.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: What Moved Markets This Week
data.description:
Listen on the go! A daily podcast of Wall Street Breakfast will be available by 8:00 a.m. on Seeking Alpha, iTunes, Spotify.

Up for a challenge? Test your knowledge on the biggest events in the investing world over the past week. Take the latest Seeking Alpha News Quiz and see how you stack up against the competition.


Wall Street ended the week substantially lower as concerns about the conflict in the Middle East lingered and oil prices stayed higher at around $100 per barrel. Meanwhile, the Federal Reserve announced its decision to keep interest rates unchanged, as widely expected.

Brent futures (CO1:COM) rose around 8.9% in the past five days to settle around $112 on Friday, while WTI Crude (CL1:COM) actually declined around 0.6% this week to around $98 per barrel. It is, however, an increase of almost 55% and 47%, respectively, from a month ago.

In addition, the Federal Reserve held interest rates steady at 3.50%-3.75% for a third straight meeting on Wednesday, in line with expectations. In its communication, the Fed cited the uncertainty of the Middle East conflict weighing on the economy.

In economic news, the February Producer Price Index came in hotter at +0.7% month-over-month, compared to the expected +0.3%, and Core PPI, excluding foods and energy, was +0.5% month-over-month vs. +0.3% consensus.

Moreover, U.S. Treasury yields rose significantly this week due to renewed concerns about the near-term path of monetary policy after the Fed’s decision to keep rates steady. The U.S. 10-Year bond yield (US10Y) was up 2.45% this week to 4.39%, while the U.S. 2-Year bond yield (US2Y) was up 4.8% to 3.91%, and the U.S. 30-Year yield (US30Y) was up 0.8% this week to 4.94%.

For the week, the S&P (SP500) lost -1.9%, while the tech-heavy Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) dipped -2.1%, and the blue-chip Dow (DJI) fell -2.1%. Read a preview of next week's major events in Seeking Alpha's Catalyst Watch.
